data.title: Pzena Large Cap Focused Value (U.S.) Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
U.S. broad market indices rose in the quarter, fueled by continued optimism around AI and the prospect of more accommodative monetary policy, which culminated in a rate cut in September. The technology sector was the strongest performer, and growth materially outperformed value. Our Large Cap Focused Value strategy rose in the quarter but underperformed the value benchmark.

The telecommunications, health care, and industrials sectors all declined in the quarter. Medical equipment manufacturer Baxter (BAX) was the largest individual detractor, after a disappointing earnings report driven by a voluntary recall related to a recent product launch and slower-than-expected recovery in IV fluid demand, which was disrupted last year by Hurricane Helene. We expect these issues to be transitory and view the stock price reaction as disproportionate relative to the magnitude of the earnings pressure, and we added to our position. Broadband provider Charter Communications (CHTR) declined after reporting higher-than-expected subscriber losses. After conducting further diligence on Charter, we concluded that the competitive pressure in the industry is likely more severe and persistent than we had expected, and we subsequently exited the position. IT services provider Cognizant (CTSH) detracted despite an encouraging earnings report, as valuations across the sector compressed due to concerns that AI may negatively impact growth prospects for tech services firms. While we continue to assess this risk, we believe the fear is likely overblown.

Financials, consumer discretionary, and consumer staples were the strongest sectors in the portfolio. Citigroup (C) was the largest individual contributor after Q2 earnings beat expectations, demonstrating broad-based revenue growth across business lines and strong operating leverage. Auto supplier Magna International (MGA:CA) rose after raising its earnings guidance, driven by self-help benefits. TE Connectivity (TEL), a supplier of industrial connectors, rallied after reporting earnings that benefitted from strong demand for products tied to AI data center buildouts.

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in PPG Industries (PPG), a leading coatings provider. PPG boasts enviable franchises across multiple coatings markets and has historically generated attractive returns on capital. The shares are currently trading at a discounted valuation due to concerns about tepid organic growth relative to peers. We see these concerns as overly discounted in the valuation and expect growth to improve over time as key end markets recover from cyclical pressure.

We added to our positions in life insurer MetLife (MET), which underperformed due to short-term earnings concerns that we view as transitory, and dialysis provider Fresenius Medical Care (FMS), which underperformed after reporting soft treatment growth. We trimmed positions in TE Connectivity, CVS Health, and Citigroup, all on strength.

Valuations remain attractive in the portfolio, which is most exposed to the health care and financials sectors.

data.title: Wasatch Emerging Markets Small Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Lower U.S. interest rates, steady enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) and an upturn in investor sentiment toward China created a positive backdrop for emerging-market small-cap stocks during most of the third quarter. Easing U.S.-China trade tensions and prospects for continued central-bank support in Latin America and Asia helped sustain risk appetite. The MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index rose 5.36% for the quarter. Trailing the benchmark, the Wasatch Emerging Markets Small Cap Fund—Investor Class (WAEMX) was down -3.46%.

Stock selection and overweight positioning in India were the biggest source of underperformance relative to the benchmark. Steep U.S. tariffs on many Indian products, imposed in response to New Delhi's purchases of Russian crude, pressured exporters and undermined confidence. Late in the quarter, U.S. President Donald Trump's sharp increase in H-1B visa fees raised concerns that inflows of remittances to India could slow, potentially weakening the rupee. Investors also feared the visa changes would disrupt the business models of services exporters that depend on the H-1B program to place engineers with overseas clients.

Softness in Indian equities contrasted with strong advances in China, a top-returning component of the Index during the third quarter. Investors reacted favorably to signs of economic stabilization, targeted policy support and a more business-friendly regulatory climate. Meanwhile, the success of Chinese startup

DeepSeek's latest AI model, together with the reversal of U.S. restrictions on access to advanced semiconductor technology, stirred optimism about the country's tech sector. Our holdings in China outgained the benchmark's positions, though the country's lower weight in the Fund limited the upside to relative results.

The U.S. Federal Reserve's pivot to monetary easing during the third quarter marked a welcome shift from the prior period, when concerns over tariff-driven inflation had kept policy on hold. Lower rates in the U.S. helped keep the dollar in check, supporting currency gains in countries that included Brazil and Mexico. The Fed's move also gave wider scope for other central banks to pursue rate cuts of their own, a prospect that created an added tailwind for emerging markets during the quarter.

Details of The Quarter

The top three contributors to Fund performance for the quarter— Shenzhen Envicool Technology Co. Ltd. , Chroma ATE, Inc. and Jentech Precision Industrial Co. Ltd. , respectively, benefited from the ongoing buildout of AI infrastructure by major hyperscalers. A Chinese company, Envicool offers precision cooling systems for data centers, a market experiencing surging demand as AI workloads drive greater heat output and density within server environments. The company also stands to gain from easing tensions between the U.S. and China over advanced semiconductor technology, which is built around high-performance chips that require specialized cooling solutions.

Chroma ATE is a Taiwanese manufacturer of automated test equipment for power electronics, semiconductors and electric-vehicle components. Sales in the company's most-recent quarter rose 17% year-over-year, while gross margin expanded to 65% from 59%. These gains reflect strong demand for Chroma ATE's testing systems as AI-related semiconductor production and electrification trends spur higher volumes and more complex testing requirements. Anticipated capital spending by clean-energy manufacturers added to investor optimism.

Jentech, also based in Taiwan, provides heat spreaders and other thermal management solutions for semiconductor chips. Demand for Jentech's products has accelerated alongside the rapid buildout of AI, machine learning and data-center infrastructure. Heat spreaders help disperse heat across a chip's surface, allowing processors to sustain performance under rising power densities. As computing workloads become more energy-intensive, effective cooling solutions have become increasingly important.

The greatest detractor from performance for the quarter was Baltic Classifieds Group PLC . Headquartered in Lithuania, the company offers online classifieds platforms for automotives, real estate, jobs and general merchandise. Baltic is experiencing a slowdown on its Estonian auto platform after the government introduced a new vehicle tax at the start of 2025. The policy change pulled forward demand, with dealers and consumers accelerating purchases in late 2024 ahead of the levy. Since then, the Estonian auto market has slowed more sharply, and for longer, than initially expected. While signs of stabilization are beginning to emerge, management doesn't expect the company's auto business in Estonia to return to year-over-year growth until early 2026.

Indian financial companies accounted for several of the Fund's other significant detractors. Among these were Cholamandalam Financial Holdings Ltd. ("Chola") and Five-Star Business Finance Ltd.

Chola offers a full spectrum of lending services through more than 1,600 branches located primarily in small and medium-sized cities and towns across India. The company has been contending with higher credit costs stemming from late-stage delinquencies in its vehicle-finance business. Management attributed the rise to transitory factors—including early monsoons, flooding and weaker industrial activity—that disrupted asset utilization and limited the ability of borrowers to generate income and repay loans. The company reaffirmed its full-year growth outlook and anticipates margin recovery in the second half of the year, which reinforces our view that recent pressures are temporary rather than structural.

Five-Star provides small-business and mortgage loans to eligible borrowers to meet their business and personal needs. Quarterly earnings were moderately weaker than expected, reflecting more modest loan growth, margin compression and higher credit costs. Some of the recent stress has come from the microfinance lending segment, which has gone through a period of rapid, unsustainable growth. We believe much of that pressure is now behind the industry as underwriting has become more disciplined and regulators have increased oversight. More important, Five-Star's emphasis on secured lending to underserved small businesses supports an attractive outlook for long-term growth. ( Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change. )

Outlook and Positioning

Stocks in India, the Fund's most heavily weighted country, underperformed other emerging markets during the third quarter as the nation found itself drawn into geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and Russia. While unfortunate, this does not diminish our favorable long-term outlook for Indian equities. India is widely regarded as one of the world's most attractive investment destinations, supported by world-leading economic growth, favorable demographics, rising urbanization and a highly educated workforce. Once the current geopolitical uncertainties and trade volatility ease—and we believe they will—we expect that equity performance in India could improve significantly.

More broadly, AI is rapidly reshaping the competitive landscape across industries and geographies. Recent breakthroughs in generative models, automation and data analytics are transforming how companies design products, reach customers and manage operations. This is not a passing trend. It's a structural shift with the potential to redraw sector boundaries, compress traditional value chains and create entirely new categories of winners and losers. For investors, the implications are profound: AI is simultaneously a catalyst for disruption and a platform for growth, and the pace of adoption will increasingly influence corporate performance, market leadership and capital flows.

Identifying long-term beneficiaries of AI is a complex undertaking. Over the past year and a half, our exposure to AI-related opportunities was somewhat limited as we focused on finding business models with the durability, growth potential and competitive advantages needed to sustain performance over time. In recent months, we've added companies to the strategy that we believe are well-positioned to benefit from the rapid changes underway.

With that foundation in place, we have a heavy travel schedule planned for the remainder of 2025. Our third trip this year to Taiwan will include more tech-oriented visits as we extend our work on AI and the value chain surrounding it. We've also scheduled travel to China, Mexico and Brazil along with a third trip to India this year. In China, our agenda will follow two tracks—one focused on technology and one dedicated to nontechnology sectors. We believe these increased research efforts will deepen our insight into the opportunities and risks ahead and could position the Fund to deliver attractive long-term investment returns.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Scott Thomas, Dan Chace, Ajay Krishnan, Kevin Unger and Anh Hoang

data.title: CLOA: Hold To Maintain The Extra Yield
data.description:
The iShares AAA CLO Active ETF (CLOA) is an ETF designed for investors who want to earn returns slightly higher than those offered by the money market, but without taking on aggressive credit risk. This fund seeks to provide steady periodic cash flows by investing in high-quality structured products (CLOs) with very low sensitivity to interest rate changes. From my current point of view, I would keep the vehicle in the portfolio if the reader already owns it, because, as we will see later, it provides a total return higher than that of 3M treasury bills, with an extra credit risk that is acceptable given the rating of the CLOs it purchases.

To properly understand ETFs, you need to understand what a CLO is. A CLO (collateralized debt obligation) is a securitized product that bundles together a large number of bank loans, mostly from over-indebted companies. This product is organized into tranches, which are ranked according to their priority of collection. The senior or highest priority tranches are those with the highest credit rating (AAA), which collect interest before the rest and only suffer losses if the junior tranches (with lower credit ratings) have defaulted. From a credit risk perspective, AAA tranches are the safest of this class. It is in this universe of securitized products that the fund invests most of its assets.

The coupons that the fund charges for the purchase of the aforementioned CLOs are mostly variable, as they are linked to the SOFR rate plus an extra yield (credit spread). This means that as interest rates change, these coupons will adjust to the new market conditions. As the interest rates to which they are referenced are adjusted frequently (every 1-3 months), the coupon also adjusts, which almost completely reduces the fund's exposure to duration risk.

Fund Overview

In the image above, we can see the DNA of the ETF we are analyzing. It currently has 373 open positions in its portfolio, which gives us an idea of the diversification carried out by the management team. In the return and risk section, we can see that the 30-day SEC yield is around 5.1-5.2%, while the dividend yield for the last 12 months is slightly higher, at around 5.4%. This tells us what kind of asset it is and how it performs. The average life of the portfolio is 3.6 years, with an effective duration of 0.11 years. This is due to the updating of the (floating) coupons, which gives the fund a similar interest rate risk to that of a money market fund.

The ETF charges a management fee of 0.20%, which is in line with the associated cost of other products in its class. From the participant's point of view, this means that the bulk of the carry is transferred to the investor and is hardly diminished by the layer of fees.

In terms of sectors, we can see that the fund's entire assets are invested in CLO products. The small negative cash position reflects very short-term liquidity adjustments (e.g., settlements or financing).

The credit quality of the fund's holdings indicates that more than 98% of its assets are in AAA-rated tranches. As can be seen in the image, there is no exposure below investment grade, which underscores the ETF's conservative bias within the CLO universe.

The Peers

Analyzing the main peers proposed by Seeking Alpha, we can observe the following differences. In terms of costs, the group is somewhat tight, with only CLOI and PMAA being slightly more expensive (0.36%). Where there are clear differences is in the size and maturity of each vehicle. The JAAA ETF is in a league of its own, with tens of billions under management and a longer track record. The ICLO, CLOI, and CLOX funds have been around a little longer than CLOA and PMAA, which were launched more recently. This means that CLOA is positioned in the market as a relatively new proposition compared to other funds with longer track records.

The 3-year return chart shows the returns of the different vehicles mentioned in the previous paragraph. Currently, the CLOI ETF line stands out above the rest, which may indicate that its approach to the CLO universe is the most aggressive of the comparable funds analyzed. This is reflected in CLOI's own holdings, which, instead of being concentrated in the AAA tranche, move to tranches such as AA, BBB, or unrated debt, significantly increasing its risk profile.

The block of strict AAA tranches is grouped in the middle zone (CLOA, ICLO, PMAA, and JAAA) with price returns of around 2%–3% in the period observed, which fits with a profile where almost all the return comes from carry and the price moves very little.

Carry vs. Cash

The Carry vs. Cash chart clearly shows why it makes sense to hold CLOA if it is already in your portfolio. Over the last year, the fund has generated a total return of around 5.6%, compared to approximately 4.3% for 3-month T-bills via TBIL. In other words, investors have captured around 120–150 basis points of extra return simply by being in AAA CLOs instead of T-bills, and they have done so with a very stable value trajectory. It is also very relevant how this extra return has been achieved, as the chart shows no major shocks (only a small dip in April 2025 caused by Trump and his tariffs). With this, we can say that the additional risk currently assumed compared to treasury bills is materializing correctly. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Thrivent High Income Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • Thrivent High Income Municipal Bond Fund outperformed the Bloomberg 65% High Grade/35% High Yield Index during the third quarter by 69 basis points; the Fund underperformed the Index by 96 basis points for the trailing 12-month period.
  • The Fund maintains an overweight to longer-dated municipal bonds which outperformed in the quarter, though underperformed for the 12 months.
  • BBB and high yield bonds underperformed investment grade bonds for the quarter and for the trailing 12 months.

Performance factors

During the third quarter of 2025, the Fund outperformed the Bloomberg 65% High Grade/35% High Yield Index, returning 3.21% vs 2.52%. Trailing 12-month underperformed with a .02% return for the Fund vs .98% for the Index. Underperformance for the year is attributed to the sharp twist effect experienced in the municipal curve, where long end rates widened on concerns of inflation, policy uncertainty, and government debt levels while the short end rallied. Outperformance in the most recent quarter is attributed to a large September parallel shift down in municipal rates, resulting in long-end outperformance.

The third quarter gave us somewhat mixed economic data, one rate cut, and a little more clarity on tariff impacts. While jobs data softened, overall unemployment has remained relatively healthy as we continue to see robust consumer spending. The Federal Reserve (Fed) instituted one 25 basis point rate cut in September, the first of the calendar year, and has indicated that two more may materialize by year end, should data support it. Key to determining future Fed action is inflation data, which remains stubbornly close to 3% versus the Fed's target of 2%, but has yet to see substantial increases from tariff activity. The Supreme Court is expected to rule on the administration's tariff policy. If the actions are deemed unlawful, alternative measures may follow, potentially leading to lower overall tariff rates.

Long end Treasury rates this year have been somewhat rangebound – particularly compared to the volatility of municipal rates. Following a dramatic twist in the municipal curve in the first half of the calendar year, where the short end rallied and the long end sold off, September brought a rally in the municipal market. Long end rates fell by about 35 basis points, compared to about 5 basis points for Treasuries, partially reversing the impact of the twist experienced in the first half of the

year. This has resulted in the municipal to Treasury ratio declining from about 95% at the start of the third quarter to 89% by the end. The September shift was largely due to moderating inflation fears, growth in demand for municipals fueled by cheaper ratios, lower supply of municipal bonds from the highs of the first half of the year, and the first Fed Funds rate cut of the year due to softening labor market data.

BBB and high yield bond performance lagged investment grade for the quarter and trailing 12 months. While this did impact performance, it is limited in comparison to the impact of interest rate shifts and duration.

Bonds with longer dated maturities often exhibit higher market price volatility, which can impact the Fund's returns. The Fund may utilize Treasury futures contracts to limit some duration risk, though Treasury futures are not a perfect hedge.

Portfolio outlook

Longer maturity tax-exempt municipal bonds continue to look attractive both in absolute and relative terms. We expect to see a better balance in supply and demand for the last quarter of 2025, which should benefit performance. The Federal Reserve is now on the path of cutting interest rates; two more 25 basis point cuts are expected in the last quarter of the year. We would expect yield levels across the curve to continue to trend lower, especially if the economic data confirms that inflation is not going to increase from the current levels. The fourth quarter began with a federal government shutdown and political uncertainty a concern for the markets. We are actively monitoring our holdings, assessing each for potential impacts of federal policy changes. The Fund continues to seek out bonds where the obligors have a sustainable financial profile and provide the investors with a strong security package.

  • U.S. fixed income market performance was broadly positive for the quarter. This primarily reflected a sentiment shift from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) toward a more dovish stance, with growing expectations for rate cuts amid signs of labor market softening and political pressure from President Donald Trump. While a 25 basis point cut was delivered in September, uncertainty remains with regards to further easing for the remainder of the year. Elsewhere, labor data was mixed: solid in June but weaker in both July and August. However, unemployment stayed low and jobless claims were relatively steady. Inflation remained above the long-term target, which prompted more caution from Fed officials. Other tailwinds included corporate earnings which exceeded expectations, and consumer spending that held firm despite signs of stress among lower-income households. AI optimism continued to also fuel market gains, though concerns about sustainability and monetization emerged. Overall, strong corporate earnings, resilient consumer demand, and anticipated Fed easing helped push returns higher, though risks remain from policy uncertainty, slowing job creation, and unresolved trade issues. Trade tensions persisted, with new tariffs imposed and ongoing negotiations with China, while legal challenges to Trump's tariff authority added complexity. 1
  • U.S. leveraged loans exhibited solid returns over the quarter, gaining +1.77%. 2 Performance was primarily driven by higher carry as well as modest compression in spreads. Within the sector, Single B loans continued to outperform, beating out both BBs and CCCs. Looking at sectors, Broadcasting and Diversified Media led the way, while Automotive sector lagged. 3 Gross issuance also accelerated, with over $370bn in volume compared to just $104bn in Q2. Default/LME activity in the loan index increased modestly; including distressed exchanges, the par-weighted U.S. loan default rate finished the third quarter at approximately 3.49%. 3

Fund Review

  • The Fund returned +1.78%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. The Fund's benchmark, the Morningstar LSTA U.S. Leveraged Loan Index*, returned +1.77% during the same period.

Past performance does not guarantee future returns.

  • Positive credit selection within the CCC rating tier was a primary contributor for the quarter. This was a combination of underweight positions to several issuers within Automobiles that underperformed after facing increased financial and liquidity challenges. Additionally, the portfolio was overweight several positions within Capital Goods and Telecommunication Services sectors that outperformed following more idiosyncratic events like increased M&A activity and strong earnings. The portfolio also benefited from a modest allocation to high yield corporates, which exhibited strong performance as yields declined.
  • Several positions within the portfolio detracted from returns relative to the index, reflected by credit selection within the Single B rating tier. These were primarily overweight positions to issuers within Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Durables sectors that underperformed. Additionally, the portfolio was underweight several issuers that exhibited positive quarterly earnings that led to outperformance for the quarter. Credit selection within Commercial Services and Software sectors also detracted, reflected by positions that lagged due to structural shifts from AI adoption.

Fund Positioning & Outlook

  • Continue to target loans with high carry.

Loan spreads have remained tight and a high percentage of the loan index now trades above par, limiting opportunities to add convexity to the Fund. In this context, we focused on adding loans that offer higher levels of carry. We believe that loans remain an attractive source of carry as yields remain elevated, which may provide more income and additional cushion in the event that spreads widen.

  • We maintained barbell approach for credit rating exposure.

We continue to hold modest overweights to BBs and CCCs while underweight Single Bs. For the quarter, we found more attractive value in high quality BBs relative to the single-B cohort where we saw an increase in aggressive repricing activity. For down-in-quality, we maintained the overweight which skewed towards shorter dated securities where we believe performance is likely to be catalyst driven. We continued to add to BB positions over the period, mainly in Energy and Utilities sectors, while trimming single-Bs are tight valuations.

  • Sector exposure remains relatively balanced.

The Fund's top overweight positions remained Utilities and Transportation, with primary additions to Commercial and Professional Services, Materials and Capital Goods sectors. We have focused on more idiosyncratic positions in these sectors and also are vigilant within certain AI disruption themes that have been prominent in the loan market. Therefore, we've reduced Software and Services, which is now a top underweight, along with Consumer Services.

  • We remain constructive on the bank loan market amid a favorable economic backdrop.

Concerns that developed in the first half of the year following the tariff shock and sharp volatility in April have largely subsided, which has helped loan performance continue to be resilient. Loan issuers have continued to be supported by solid fundamentals, which were solid through the fastest Fed hiking cycle in over 40 years. And following multiple rate cuts by the Fed from peak level in 2024, we expect underlying credit metrics of loan issuers, such as interest and fixed charge coverage ratios, to steadily improve. We also continue to see a supportive technical, particularly on the demand side with continued demand from CLO creation. However, we are wary of several potential headwinds such as stickier inflation or unexpectedly strong data that could prompt a more hawkish Fed stance, as well as renewed geopolitical tensions or weaker-than-expected macro indicators that could challenge the current outlook.

Credit Quality Distribution

data.title: Netstreit: Reliable Yield And Solid Expansion, Yet Shares Appear Fully Priced
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

NETSTREIT (NTST) is a REIT focusing on net lease single-tenant properties across the US, with a portfolio focused on healthy e-commerce resistant tenants, formed in late 2019 and developing its portfolio significantly since that time.

At this point, the stock's potential seems to be properly priced in, with the opportunity diminishing significantly since the beginning of the year to the point where adding or starting a new position doesn't seem justified anymore. It's definitely a solid company to keep an eye on, but the risk-reward is no longer appealing enough to call this a buy, in my opinion.

The stock recovered quite a bit from the beginning of the year, returning to around the value from the IPO thanks to internal developments, ramped-up leverage and growth initiatives, and some improvements in the macroenvironment, being up about 30% YTD.

As mentioned, the company has a portfolio with many high-quality tenants, including Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), 7-Eleven, Dollar General (DG), and more - similar to their larger peers, although a bit more concentrated - for a total of 114 tenants (46.9% investment grade; 62.1% investment grade & investment grade profile %) and 721 investments. Also worth mentioning is that the company’s occupancy rate stands at an almost perfect 99.9%, with a weighted average lease term of 9.9 years.

As we can see, the company reported a solid $28.05 million AFFO in Q3, reaching $81.76 million YTD. The company’s AFFO per share guidance for 2025 is $1.29 to $1.31, meaning about $108.52 million based on the current number of shares outstanding, which is decent for a $1.54 billion market cap, placing them at a P/AFFO ratio of ~14.2.

As we can see, the company is very active in terms of acquisitions, with 50 investments in Q3 alone, for a total of 107 total investments YTD, as well as 60 dispositions this year, bringing the net investment activity to $237.05 million YTD, and a significant chunk of that being in Q3. They also increased their net investment guidance to $350 million to $400 million, with $690 million in recently raised capital as they continue to develop.

Of course, as they are an REIT and have to pay most of what they make back to us, they have few ways to fund these moves. Other than the asset recycling they’re doing, issuing stock and debt are their main source of external capital, with 12.42 million shares sold during their July 2025 forward equity offering, for example.

Again, this is normal, and it’s part of the business model, and the ultimate goal is to deliver long-term per share accretion. Don’t expect this to be visible instantly, though, as we have to be aware of the current environment and its implications for a growing company, as I mention below.

Financially, based on NTST’s latest report, we see a normal position for a REIT, with a decent amount of cash and equivalents and a significant rise in debt this year to fund their development. In this case, the values recorded on the balance sheet are somewhat more accurate compared to other REITs, as these assets were recently added since the company is new, although they still accumulated depreciation and the market values likely still rose, so the NAV is probably higher. This is different for older REITs, as their PB ratio is likely higher than a company whose assets have been recently recorded.

NTST also recently secured $450 million in additional financing commitments and amendments to its existing credit facilities. As we can see, their interest rates are not that high given the current environment, being relatively unlucky in terms of IPO and debt issuing timing, with the IPO valuation potentially being hurt by the pandemic (August 2020) and the debt more than doubling from 2022, so most of it was affected by the Fed’s rate hikes.

As we can see, their debt is spread well into the future, with no significant maturity, and, except for the $8 million in mortgage notes due in 2027, there’s no maturity all the way until 2028, allowing the company to wait and prepare before having to refinance, which can be great if rate cuts advance.

Shareholder Returns

The ~4.7% dividend yield costs them roughly $72.4 million per year, placing the payout ratio at roughly 66.72%, which is definitely solid for this yield. Especially given their potential improvements going forward, it wouldn’t be impossible to see them grow this to something closer to their peers’ 70% to 80% payout ratios, with an 80% payout ratio, for example, representing a ~5.64% yield while they’d still have some flexibility to develop and maintain a solid balance sheet. They had a couple of increases around the half of the year before, but there’s definitely better potential if they want to, although the company is currently in a growth/accumulation stage rather than a traditional cash cow.

Risks & Opportunities

The key catalyst for NTST and especially this asset class will be rate cuts. I’ll mention again that the business model depends a lot on the difference between their cost of debt and the returns on their investments, meaning that a higher cost of debt fundamentally affects the company’s returns, especially for a young company like this that had to issue most of its debt during the high-rate environment seen recently. In NTST’s case, their cash yield on their recent investments is 7.4%, which is not a high premium above their over 5% interest on the debt they issue.

On top of that, their tenants should also benefit from better financing conditions and higher consumer discretionary budgets, so rate cuts can help in several ways, especially if NTST continues to expand into other types of tenants than dollar stores, potentially benefitting from a recovery. Especially since rate cuts would also lower treasury yields over time and potentially lead to better risk-adjusted valuations, this would allow NTST to develop more efficiently through a combination of debt and issuing stock, which is an environment where this asset class thrives.

But again, don’t count on rate cuts to instantly fix everything, as it can take several years to see this being reflected in their valuation and cost structure through refinancing. With the US Government recently going through what became its longest shutdown in history before reopening, there’s no wonder that the market has been quite pessimistic recently, especially due to the effect it can have on the near-term future of rate cuts.

This also caused the BLS to halt its data collection and publishing (with a new release only in November), meaning that the Fed has less up-to-date data for its next meeting at a time when uncertainty around tariffs, inflation, and unemployment remained elevated.

One advantage that NTST has is that their size and strategy allow them to take advantage of smaller opportunities that would otherwise not even move the needle for larger peers, taking advantage of what they call inefficiently priced assets that come with less competition. This can allow them to continue their aggressive expansion while also offering the potential to eventually move the needle for a bigger player that could buy their portfolio as a whole.

I don’t think being bought is ideal, especially for long-term investors, unless the buyer is also solid or the deal is very good, but this can offer a safety net that would prevent them from falling too much. Again, they have almost the same tenants as their larger peers, the occupancy rate is almost perfect, and they could even represent the “high-growth” arm of a larger company if they decide to keep their strategy, which can have significantly more potential if they get access to the massive capital, credit rating, etc. of a big peer.

Still, NTST’s management will have to navigate this environment carefully, even though it’s true that all they saw since the company’s inception was an unpredictable, volatile, and then high-rate environment, and they kept developing well even through this.

To some degree, their tenants are the reason behind it, with dollar stores like DG actually growing since they thrive in this type of environment, and I’m looking forward to seeing how they’ll perform during a good environment for this asset class - but until then, things will continue to be uncertain.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers like Realty Income (O), NNN REIT (NNN), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT), and W. P. Carey (WPC), we see overall average ratios from almost every point of view, and, as mentioned, the PB is more attractive here since the company’s equity is likely closer to the NAV thanks to the assets being recorded recently on the balance sheet, so they’re closer to their “real” market value.

The dividend yield is not that attractive compared to these peers, but it’s true that their payout ratio is the smallest in the group, and getting them to the same payout would place NTST very close to these yields.

Valuation

Starting with the expected AFFO of ~$108.52 million per year, let’s assume a 6.5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to a combination of lower interest rates combined with their expansion initiatives and investments, followed by 4.5% for the subsequent 5 years as they’d be approaching a more mature state, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Let’s go for a WACC of 8% (above my estimated 7.68% using a cost of equity of 8.9% and 5.9% cost of debt), with $1.065 billion in net debt and 83.48 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $2.488 billion, an equity value of $1.423 billion, and an intrinsic value of $17.05 per share.

Conclusions

I rate NTST a Hold now, as the company seems to be trading around a fair value after the significant jump since the lows from early 2025, despite their growth initiatives and solid fundamentals. I don’t see something that would justify buying at these levels from a value point of view, but they’re likely a solid option for income investors that want a bit of diversity and perhaps higher growth potential than the classic O, NNN, WPC, and the rest.

I believe there are better options that I recently rated Strong Buys, such as Getty Realty (GTY), Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE), and more that you can check out here.

data.title: Mercury General: From Red Flag To Reluctant Buy
data.description:
The relationship between Mercury (MCY) and me is a long story. Not a loving one, but not a hateful one either. I would rather name it a neutral, unbiased, analytical relationship.

I discovered MCY in 2019 when I started to be interested in dividend insurance aristocrats. In my first review of the company, I pointed out the company's weaknesses:

  • A weak underwriting performance, with a combined ratio ranging from around 100% and even above.
  • An anemic dividend growth (0.4% per year at that time). This almost nil growth was still sufficient for the company to retain "dividend aristocrat" status, but it was not a positive indicator of dividend sustainability.

I changed my mind quickly in 2020, downgrading Mercury to "SELL". From 2020 to 2023, my thesis did not change, and the dividend cut decided in 2022 was the expected consequence of years of weak underwriting management.

After years of underperformance, Mercury finally saw a therapist—pricing - pricing discipline - and started working on itself. The 2024 results showed that the introspection and this inner work bore fruit: a 96.1% combined ratio, or a 9.7 point improvement.

January 2025 wildfires were a stress test. And Mercury didn't ghost me this time. Mercury absorbed the losses with a Q2 2025 underwriting performance rebound, and the stock performance was more than promising, with a year-to-date stock gain of circa 30%. Still not marriage material, but at least now Mercury shows up for the date - on time, and with better loss picks.

On November 4th, Mercury General posted its third-quarter results, and the market appreciated them, as the underwriting performance was steady and the full losses incurred during the first quarter were absorbed. The workout routine, started in 2024 with a pause in Q1 2025, has shown its effects on Mercury. The insurance carrier appears to be steadier than two or three years ago, even though the underwriting performance remains below better peers.

Q3 2025 Performance: Lovely Surprising

During my last date with Mercury General, I expected an underwriting income of $85.2 million for the third quarter. With a quarterly combined ratio of 87%, Mercury General generated a pre-tax underwriting gain of $185.1 million, or $100 million more than I expected.

On a year-to-date basis, the company posted an underwriting gain of $39.8 million, vs. $88.7 million last year in the same period.

The year-over-year underwriting gain reduction was fully driven by the Q1 2025 losses. Without this major event (which, anyway, could happen - that's why insurance coverages exist), Mercury General would be even brighter and sexier.

Valuation Corner: The Mixed Feelings

Mercury's shareholder equity stood at approximately $2.2 billion as of September 30, 2025, and the company reported a net income of $338 million, or $6.11 per share, over the first nine months of the year.

In comparison, the Q4 EPS in 2024 and 2023 were $1.82 and $3.46, respectively.

Usually, insurers are valued either through the P/E or P/B multiples. The old-fashioned valuation techniques are sometimes adequate. Historically, investors have been willing to pay an average of 14.7x earnings and 1.48x book value for MCY.

Currently, the market capitalization is around $4.93 billion. It means that the current P/B is around 2.2. On the P/E side, if the Q4 2025 EPS ranges from $2 to $3, the FY2025 EPS will land at $8.11 to $9.11, or a forward P/E of 9.8 to 11.

We (or a least, I) face a dilemma. On P/B only, Mercury is priced for perfection or even higher. On P/E, Mercury is a cheap, under-the-radar company.

Who should you trust? The P/B guy, who assumes that Mercury is a Drama Queen? Or the P/E guy, who sees the diamond in Cinderella? Maybe P/E is the trustworthy guy - the one who sees beyond past drama, and bets that the bride will keep glowing.

What? A Rating Update?!

Mercury, like anyone you date, has flaws. My ex-wife used to say "lesser qualities". Let's be honest and talk about Mercury's shortcomings:

  • Mercury is focused on the California market, which suffers from two defects for an insurer: (1) recurring catastrophe events, which could be massive, and (2) a regulator obsessed with consumer protection.
  • From a line-of-business perspective, the insurer is focused on the retail insurance market, where the competition is massive. Mercury is not a price-maker on its own markets. Policyholders are always ready to cheat with a cheaper insurer.
  • The year-to-date underwriting gain is modest—just - just 1%, and almost entirely driven by the excellent third-quarter results. An ugly fourth-quarter performance might make investors run away.
  • Another issue is the $527 million subrogation recovery booked from the Eaton fire. This has been reserved, not received. And the choice of the word matters - it’s an accounting bet, not a wire transfer.

On the bright side:

  • The 55% recovery estimates expected by Mercury don't look over-optimistic. In the footnotes of the Q3 2025 report, Mercury pointed to the following:

Although SCE has not admitted that its equipment caused the Eaton fire, significant evidence indicates that SCE's equipment was the cause of the Eaton fire. In addition, SCE has disclosed that it is probable that SCE will incur material losses from the Eaton fire and entered into a negotiated agreement without litigation with one insurance company to pay 52% of the losses incurred.

Mercury’s assumption falls within historical ranges: for other California wildfires caused by utilities (2017–2024), settlements without admission of fault typically covered 55% to 70% of insured losses. SCE also has access to the California Wildfire Fund, which adds further credibility to Mercury’s recovery estimate.

  • Excluding catastrophe effects and prior year development, the loss ratio improved by 3.2 percentage points to 64.3%, mostly due to the rate changes. That’s a strong indicator that Mercury’s pricing and underwriting fixes are working, despite the “bad luck” of January’s wildfires.

Final Thoughts

I hesitated a lot before changing my mind regarding Mercury General. I could even say I am still hesitating, and it's not false modesty. The California-based insurance carrier still faces challenges:

  • The Californian market remains exposed to natural disasters, and Mercury is not best equipped to counter the volatility of the natural events.
  • The third-quarter results could be even seen "too good to be true".
  • Mercury's lack of diversification is another concern. A fundamental principle in insurance is mutualization and diversification. You can diversify your portfolio geographically or by offering non-correlated products. Ideally, you do both. Mercury mostly offers home and auto insurance in California.

Like Safety (SAFT) (which I used to compare to Mercury), size is an issue. Some may say size doesn’t matter. I’m sorry, but it does - at least in the insurance industry. When you're too small, you suffer. The bigger and more disciplined you are, the better your chances of surviving a 1-in-100-year black swan event.

But - and here comes the twisted conclusion - I’m also surprised by the company’s ongoing efforts. I can’t ignore them. Mercury has suffered, and still suffers. But Mercury has improved. It’s not the “best pupil” in the insurance industry class - and it never will be. But effort deserves recognition. So let’s call it what it is: a rating upgrade to a neutral “BUY”.

data.title: Strategy: Confidence Is Cracking (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Strategy (MSTR) have been in freefall since early October. MSTR falling in price is not something that I'm particularly surprised by given the declines in Bitcoin (BTC-USD). I've been stressing caution through my MSTR coverage on Seeking Alpha for well over a year. Perhaps more important than the MSTR declines, Strategy's preferred stocks (STRK)(STRF)(STRD)(STRC) are now also collapsing. This directly challenges my prior thinking and in this update, I'll detail why I think the market has lost confidence in Strategy's Bitcoin flywheel.

The Preferred Stocks Are Unraveling

As of writing, each of Strategy's preferred stocks are down significantly during intraday trading. These stocks are down between 3-8%. For income-investments, these are absolutely massive moves. In recent months, I've made the case for why I felt one of Strategy's income-based products could conceivably do well when Bitcoin's price went down. In reality, the opposite has been true and I'm now forced to reassess my thinking.

While I have not been bullish either the STRK or STRD shares in the past, I have been bullish the STRF shares. To be clear, I do still hold STRF shares. But I also see some serious problems that I think warrant trimming that position. First, STRD and STRK are now trading below IPO price. Beyond that, STRF has fallen below $100 per share for the first time since June and is currently at its lowest price level since May. What this means is that every single dollar-based preferred stock that Strategy offers is currently trading below par. Since MSTR shares are also trading at a size-able discount to mNAV, it puts Strategy in a very precarious position with limited options as the company's December dividend payments due date quickly approaches.

The Problem

For those who have followed either my work or the stock itself, this shouldn't be a surprise. Strategy does not actually generate positive income from operating its software business. We have to strip out gains or losses on digital assets that were purchased years prior to get a real assessment of the company's software business operations. On a trailing twelve month basis, Strategy's adjusted operating loss is over $42 million:

Millions Q3-24 Q4-24 Q1-25 Q2-25 Q3-25
Revenue $116.1 $120.7 $111.1 $114.5 $128.7
Cost of Revenue $34.3 $34.2 $34.0 $35.8 $38.0
SG&A $68.9 $72.4 $68.1 $70.2 $68.1
R&D $33.3 $25.7 $24.4 $24.1 $22.6
Operating Income -$20.4 -$11.6 -$15.4 -$15.6 $0.0

Source: Seeking Alpha

The only reason Strategy has been able to buy Bitcoin to the degree that it has is due to the company's ability to raise capital through various instruments including convertible notes, preferred stocks, and good old-fashioned common stock dilution. Raising money to buy BTC was easy when MSTR traded at a significant premium to mNAV.

That is no longer the case and we can see in the chart above how weekly acquisitions have fallen in line with MSTR mNAV compression. Anecdotally, it is quite obvious that MSTR shareholder appetite for more dilution of the common is virtually gone judging by sentiment online. But it's important to keep in mind that 89% of the Bitcoin purchase capital raised since preferred stock ATMs launched in March has actually come from the common ATM.

Strategy can just switch to issuing more preferred stock through ATMs then? Maybe not, actually. To illustrate why, let's look at one of Strategy's recent Bitcoin acquisition filings:

I've crudely circled the nominal value versus net proceeds for each of the four preferred ATM raises during the week of November 3rd. Strategy was really only able to utilize STRF and STRC because those where the two products that it could use to generate greater proceeds from the stock issuance than the nominal value of the obligation it was creating. That was not the case for STRK or STRD, which is why those raises were so much smaller by comparison. The point is, Strategy does not want to issue $6.4 million in preferred stocks to generate just $5.5 million in proceeds. It would much rather do the opposite. Now it can't.

At the end of September, Strategy had $54.3 million in cash and equivalents. Since then, the company has raised about $318 million through its ATM programs and purchased almost $292 million in BTC with that capital. That leaves, at best, $80 million or so in cash to pay out nearly $150 million in dividend obligations before the end of the year.

Preferred Class Shares Dividend/Shr Divis Due EOY
STRK (Due Dec) 13,974,912 $2.00 $27,949,824
STRF (Due Dec) 12,676,383 $2.50 $31,690,958
STRD (Due Dec) 12,344,308 $3.06 $37,724,205
STRC (Due Nov+Dec) 29,587,063 $0.88 $51,777,360
Total Dividends Due by EOY $149,142,347

Source: Strategy, Analyst's Calculations, estimated dividend payments

To be sure, Strategy could simply not pay the STRD dividends in December; though I suspect doing so what irreparably damage the company's ability to continue raising capital should it wish to continue doing so in the future. But even if it doesn't pay STRD shareholders in December, the company would still have a dividend shortfall through the other three products.

Strategy's Next Move

There are a variety of ways Executive Chairman Michael Saylor and company could play the precarious situation that Strategy now finds itself in. But I think these three are the most straight forward:

  1. Keep raising capital through preferred stocks
  2. Sell Bitcoin to pay dividends
  3. Continue diluting common stock holders

I think option 1 is unlikely due to what I just laid out in the section above. Strategy cannot tap the preferred ATMs while all four of them are under par without severely damaging trust in his income products, in my opinion. However, if STRF recovers from current levels and re-takes par, we could see Strategy begin to lean on that instrument a bit more despite it's relatively small ATM. Option 2 is also unlikely because it goes counter to what Saylor has been saying for years. The time to sell BTC to pay the dividends was a month ago when Bitcoin was well over $110 per coin. Given the weakness in the market, Strategy selling BTC could trigger a deeper cascade lower in the asset. Which would result in bigger problems for the company. Option 3 seems like the easiest, 'kick the can down the road' path. Thus, it seems to me to be the most likely.

Closing Summary

Again, I have to stress that Strategy had already been heavily relying on MSTR dilution to fund purchases to this point. Given the market dynamics currently on display in the preferred stocks, Strategy's ability to raise through its income products going forward is likely minimal. Frankly, I think the market is actually daring the company to sell Bitcoin to pay its preferred obligations in December. Furthermore, at a basic mNAV of 0.9, one could argue it behooves the company to sell Bitcoin to buy back its common stock to close its own mNAV discount. While I highly doubt buying back MSTR is something the company is considering doing, I have to imagine there is at least some internal discussion about buying back some of the preferred stocks while they're under par to minimize the company's perpetual dividend obligations. I could certainly be wrong for being bullish STRF earlier this year. But I don't think I'm going to be wrong on continuing to avoid MSTR.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: Positive Q3 Results, But Valuation And Technical Concerns Appear (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of Amprius Technologies (AMPX). I previously wrote about AMPX back in August 2025. The stock increased by an impressive 45% since then. This significantly outperformed the S&P 500's (SPY) 3% gain over the same time.

The company's Q3 2025 earnings report provides positive insights into Amprius' operating health. I will also provide updates to AMPX's valuation and technical outlooks.

Positive Q3 2025 Results

Amprius recently reported positive results for Q3 2025. The company achieved yoy revenue growth of 173% to $21.4 million, which beat analysts' estimates by $4.62 million. The revenue gains were attributed to the addition of new customers and from large orders from existing customers. Amprius shipped its lithium batteries to 159 customers. Seventy-nine of those were repeat customers, while 80 were new customers.

Geographically, 75% of AMPX's Q3 revenue came from outside the United States. Amprius' customer diversification supports steady growth even with uncertainties such as tariffs and customer delays during the government shutdown.

Amprius got a major win with a $35 million purchase order from a leading UAS manufacturer. This is a follow-up order from the same customer who placed a $15 million order earlier this year. This is just an example of the types of repeat customers that Amprius has.

The company derived 75% of revenue from the aviation market. This was led by unmanned aerial systems [UAS]. The remaining 25% of revenue came from the light electric vehicle market [LEVs].

Growth Catalysts

Amprius believes that electric aerospace is on the verge of a multiyear transformation driven by defense and commercial demand for a new era of AI-driven autonomy. This market is estimated to grow from $40 billion to $50 billion today to $80 billion by the end of the decade according to McKinsey. About 10% of that market and 10% of the drone's materials are for batteries.

Recent government actions are driving growth for the drone market. Executive orders have been written this year to promote the use of domestic drones. Another driver is proposed changes to the Beyond Visual Line of Sight Rules [BVLOS] that the U.S. Federal Aviation Authority is debating. The new rules are expected to reform outdated regulations that held innovators back while increasing sky safety. Amprius expects the new rules to unlock the use of drones in the United States.

The One Big Beautiful Bill is estimated to allocate $10 billion to defense and unmanned systems. AMPX expects to benefit from this funding as it should create more demand for the company's lithium batteries for use in unmanned systems.

Amprius has been developing a relationship with Amazon (AMZN). Amprius was chosen for the inaugural Amazon Devices Climate Tech Accelerator cohort in July 2025. Amprius advanced to the assessment phase. This involves comprehensive testing of AMPX's customer value proposition, feasibility, sustainability impact, and supply chain readiness. The company will share updates on this progress as it develops.

AMPX's customer wins demonstrate their ability to scale up to meet volume purchase orders. The company expects this to continue to increase as Amprius adds to its customer base.

Amprius has the advantage of offering the most complete available portfolio of silicon anode materials in the industry. This gives the company a competitive advantage as AMPX's batteries have high energy density, attractive charging times, safety, and range. Amprius believes that they offer unmatched performance for commercially available batteries.

Valuation

Since Amprius is not expected to be profitable for a few more years, I use the price/sales ratio for the valuation. AMPX is trading at 8.5x expected sales of $164.5 million for 2026. This is higher than the price/sales of 5.4x that AMPX was trading at back in August when I last wrote about the company. Amprius is also trading much higher than the sector median forward price/sales of 1.7.

It is possible that the market continues to award AMPX with a premium valuation due to the company's strong above-average revenue growth. AMPX is expected to grow revenue at a strong pace of 199% in 2025 and follow that up with 128% growth in 2026 according to consensus estimates. The revenue growth rate is expected to return to a double-digit pace of about 30% in 2027.

The high revenue growth rates can be achieved if Amprius continues to be awarded orders from existing customers while acquiring new ones. Continued strong government defense spending for unmanned aircraft would also provide a tailwind for Amprius' strong revenue growth.

Technical Perspective

Amprius' daily chart above shows the stock consolidating after the strong run-up in price this year. The stock pulled back from the 52-week high of $16 which was reached in mid-October. The RSI is currently in bearish territory as it moved below 50. The MACD is also bearish as the blue line crossed below the red signal line and the histogram bars are red.

This price action looks like a profit-taking pullback as the selling looks controlled in a consolidating pattern. It is not a sharp pullback or a cause for concern in my opinion.

It could be the AMPX's premium valuation keeping the stock price suppressed. However, I do think the market can continue to award the stock a premium valuation for AMPX's above-average growth. It appears that the stock just needs a profit-taking pullback to make the valuation a little more attractive before buying pressure overtakes the selling pressure.

The stock could continue to drop from here. Although, I don't think the selling will be too steep. The Q3 earnings report was positive and the company news flow also remains positive.

The next catalyst could be news of new lucrative orders from new or existing customers. Revenue estimate upgrades could be another catalyst for the stock. Revenue upgrades could help lower the forward price/sales ratio. Another catalyst could be a well-defined technical bottom for the stock. The bottom and a new upward trend would be indicated by the blue MACD line crossing back above the red signal line while the histogram bars turn back to green.

The Risks for the Stock

Changing battery technology could be a risk for Amprius. For example, the use of solid state batteries is being developed for use in unmanned aircraft and other applications. Solid state batteries have an advantage over lithium-ion batteries as they are considered safer, have faster charging capabilities, have a higher energy density, and provide a longer lifespan. Solid state batteries can provide longer flight times for drones. Amprius may need to develop this technology or improve its current technology to remain competitive in future years.

Amprius Technologies remains unprofitable. There is no guarantee that the company will be able to turn a profit in the future. The company is on track to reach profitability in 2027 according to analysts' estimates. This can be achieved as Amprius gains economies of scale to drive down costs. Plus, AMPX should get a significant gross margin boost from its high revenue growth. The company did increase the gross margin from a negative (65%) in Q3 2024 to a positive 15.5% in Q3 2025. Failure to reach profitability at the expected time could result in a sell-off for the stock.

Competition remains an ongoing risk for Amprius. The company competes with SES AI (SES) who produces lithium batteries for EVs, drones, robotics, etc. AMPX also competes with QuantumScape (QS) who produces lithium batteries for robotics and other electronics. QuantumScape is also developing solid state battery technology. Other solid state battery developers include: Panasonic (OTCPK:PCRFF) (OTCPK:PCRHY), Samsung SDI (OTCPK:SSNLF), Factorial, and CATL. The solid state batteries could be mass produced in the late 2020s. These competitors could take market share away from Amprius.

Long-Term Outlook for Amprius Technologies

Amprius currently offers the market attractive and effective battery technology for use in unmanned aircraft and other applications. The company continues to get orders from existing customers while acquiring new ones. Recent U.S. government legislation should provide a tailwind for AMPX's technology.

I like Amprius' strong revenue growth rate. This should help the company reach profitability within about two years. We will have to watch how the battery technology evolves through the remainder of the decade. Amprius will need to continue to advance its battery technology to compete with new technologies like solid state batteries.

Since the valuation increased since my last article and the stock remains in a consolidation phase or pullback, I'm lowering my rating to a 'HOLD'. An improved valuation along with a better technical set-up would make me reconsider this rating.

data.title: Permian Resources: Excellent Cost And Production Performance Continues In Q3 2025
data.description:
Permian Resources (PR) reported excellent Q3 2025 results, with a 6% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil production and a 7% quarter-over-quarter increase in total production.

It also increased its full-year production guidance by 2%, which was mainly driven by continued well outperformance.

Permian's cost performance was strong, with a 6% quarter-over-quarter decrease in controllable cash costs per BOE and Q3 2025 D&C costs per lateral foot that were down -11% compared to 2024.

Permian has continued to work on natural gas marketing efforts and expects to sell most of its natural gas production into premium markets (such as the Gulf Coast) by 2027.

The strong production and cost performance, as well as the natural gas marketing efforts, have resulted in a $1 increase to my estimate of Permian's value. I now estimate its value at $18.50 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

Q3 2025 Results

Permian Resources reported excellent Q3 2025 results, reporting a 6% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil production, bringing that to 186,937 barrels of oil per day. Permian's total production increased by approximately 7% quarter-over-quarter, reaching an average of 410,225 BOEPD in Q3 2025.

While Permian has also been active with various acquisitions, I'd estimate that around 3% of its quarter-over-quarter production growth was due to acquisitions and 3% to 4% production growth was organic. Permian noted that it continued to see outperformance with its new wells.

Permian performed well with respect to costs in addition to its strong production performance. It mentioned that its Q3 2025 D&C costs were around $725 per lateral foot, down 11% from 2024 levels.

Permian's controllable cash costs per BOE decreased by 6% quarter-over-quarter, ending up at $7.36 per BOE. This was its best quarterly performance on that metric during 2025 and is towards the lower end of its full-year guidance for $7.25 to $8.25 per BOE.

Permian focused mainly on debt reduction during the quarter, reducing its debt by approximately $460 million. It repaid $287 million in 5.375% unsecured notes due 2026 at par and redeemed $170 million in 3.25% convertible notes due 2028. As the conversion price for the convertible notes was approximately $5.56, noteholders exercised their right to convert the notes into 30.6 million Class A common shares.

Permian still has related capped call transactions in place that should reduce the effective dilution from conversion. It also repurchased 2.3 million shares for $30 million during the quarter and paid its typical quarterly base dividend of $0.15 per share.

In terms of acquisitions, Permian added 5,500 net leasehold acres and 2,400 net royalty acres in Q3 2025 for $180 million in total consideration (over approximately 250 small transactions). Most of the acquired acreage was in New Mexico and is relatively lightly developed overall (with approximately 800 BOEPD in current production). This brings Permian's acquisition spending to over $800 million so far in 2025.

Natural Gas Marketing Efforts

Permian mentioned that it has firm capacity on long-distance pipelines and out of basin sales agreements that will help it sell 330 MMCF per day of natural gas at premium prices (compared to Waha) in 2026. These agreements increase to around 700 MMCF per day in 2028 natural gas volumes.

Permian estimates that this will result in a $1 per Mcf improvement in its realized prices relative to Waha in 2026, increasing its 2026 free cash flow by over $100 million.

The expected Waha differential is more modest in 2027 based on strip, so the improvement in realized prices relative to Waha may not be as high then. However, the agreements should still ensure that minimal realized natural gas prices are a thing of the past for Permian. It realized only $0.78 per Mcf for its natural gas (before hedges) during the first three quarters of 2025, and this was even lower in 2024.

Updated Guidance

As a result of its strong production performance, Permian increased its full-year 2025 production guidance for the second time. It now expects 394,000 BOEPD in 2025 production at its guidance midpoint, which is a 2% increase from its prior guidance. It also expects approximately 181,500 barrels per day in oil production, which is also a 2% increase from its prior guidance around oil production. Most of this guidance increase is from organic growth.

Permian left the rest of its full-year guidance unchanged, although it does look to be on track to end up in the lower half of its guidance around controllable cash costs per BOE.

Based on Permian's updated full-year guidance, it expects roughly 407,000 BOEPD in Q4 2025 production, including around 187,375 barrels per day of oil production. This would be roughly flat quarter-over-quarter oil production growth along with a slight -1% quarter-over-quarter decline in total production.

Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Permian's value by $1, from $17.50 per share to $18.50 per share.

This continues to assume long-term prices (after 2026) of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Permian's estimated value has improved due to its continued production outperformance (mostly driven by its newer wells) and its natural gas marketing efforts.

Permian has been reducing its exposure to Waha prices and should sell most of its natural gas production in 2027 and 2028 into the Gulf Coast and Dallas-Fort Worth markets.

Around 29% of Permian's production is natural gas, and it has been hampered at times in the past by realizing close to zero for that production when Waha prices have bottomed out.

Conclusion

Permian Resources has executed very well, delivering outperformance on both operating costs and production in Q3 2025. It has also reduced its D&C costs per lateral foot by 11% compared to 2024 and increased its firm transport and sales agreements to cover most of its natural gas production by 2027. This results in most of its natural gas being sold into premium markets instead of at Waha prices (which are sometimes near zero or even negative).

I have increased my estimate of Permian's value to $18.50 per share in a scenario with current strip prices until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.
